title: Cheese scones
subtitle: Mature cheddar, mustard powder, paprika.
excerpt: Plain-scone dough enriched with grated mature cheddar, English mustard powder, and a pinch of paprika. Stamped and baked tall, eaten warm split with butter alongside a bowl of soup. Makes eight.
sourceNotes: Cheese scones are a twentieth-century English adaptation of the plain Beeton scone, popularised by domestic-economy schools and WI cookbooks in the 1920s and 30s.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze cooked, defrost at room temperature, refresh in a 180°C oven for 5 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily; two trays at a time in a fan oven.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten warm. Dough can sit for an hour on a tray in the fridge before baking.

content.text: A standard plain-scone dough with two-thirds of the way through the rubbing-in stage, 150 g of grated mature cheddar tipped in along with a teaspoon of English mustard powder, a pinch of paprika, and an extra pinch of salt. Bound with cold milk, patted, stamped, brushed with milk and topped with an extra blanket of cheese, and baked at 220°C for 14 minutes.

The cheese has to be sharp. Mild cheddar makes a bland scone; mature or extra-mature gives the savoury depth. Mustard powder amplifies the cheese without showing up as mustard on the palate. A teaspoon of paprika on top before baking colours the crust to deep amber.

items.prepNote: mature, grated; plus 30 g extra for tops

content.text: Heat the oven to 220°C (200°C fan). Line a baking tray with parchment.
Rub in the butter, mix the dry

Sift {{self-raising-flour}} of self-raising flour, {{baking-powder}} of baking powder, {{english-mustard-powder}} of English mustard powder, {{paprika-sweet}} of sweet paprika, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt into a large bowl. Add {{unsalted-butter}} of cold cubed butter. Rub in with fingertips until the mixture looks like fine breadcrumbs. Stir in 150 g of the {{cheddar}} of grated cheddar.
Pour in {{whole-milk}} of cold milk. Stir gently with a butter knife; bring together with your hands. Don't knead.
Pat the dough on a lightly floured worktop to 3 cm thick. Stamp with a 6 cm round cutter pushed straight down. Eight rounds. Sit on the tray with a 4 cm gap between each.
Top with cheese, dust with paprika
Brush the tops with extra milk. Scatter the reserved 30 g of cheddar over the tops. Dust with a pinch of paprika.
Bake 14 to 16 minutes until tall and the cheese tops are deep amber. Cool 5 minutes on a rack. Split warm with the side of a thumb.

items.fix: Mature or extra-mature cheddar only. The mustard powder amplifies whatever the cheese is, so weak cheese gives bland scones.
Middle shelf. Cover with foil for the last 4 minutes if it's browning too fast.
Bring together quickly; don't knead. Pat, don't roll.

items.cause: Butter too warm or oven too cool.
items.fix: Use cold butter; chill the cut dough for 10 minutes before baking. Oven at 220°C from cold.

content.text: Cheese scones are the British savoury answer to the plain tea scone. They sit alongside a bowl of soup, get split and toasted for breakfast with bacon inside, or arrive in a basket for a savoury afternoon tea. Eat warm; a cold cheese scone is sad.
